title: PM Models to Know
summary: A quick-reference catalogue of the thinking models PMBOK Guide 7/8 expects you to recognise and match to a situation.

sections.heading: What a model is
sections.body:
PMBOK Guide 7/8 defines a model as a thinking strategy: a simplified way to explain a process or phenomenon so you can reason about it and choose a response. You are not asked to apply every model in depth, but to recognise the right lens for a situation.

This guide focuses on models the app does not cover elsewhere. Tuckman, Thomas-Kilmann conflict modes, motivation theories and stakeholder salience each have their own fuller guide; see the closing section.
sections.tip: Exam mantra: match the model to the context, do not force one model onto every problem.

sections.heading: Complexity models: Cynefin and Stacey
sections.body:
Cynefin sorts a situation into five domains, each calling for a different response:
- Clear (obvious): cause and effect are plain. Sense-categorise-respond, apply best practice.
- Complicated: knowable but needs expertise. Sense-analyse-respond, apply good practice.
- Complex: cause and effect only clear in hindsight. Probe-sense-respond, let practice emerge through safe-to-fail experiments.
- Chaotic: no clear cause and effect, act first. Act-sense-respond to establish order.
- Disorder: the centre, where you do not yet know which domain you are in.

Stacey matrix plots agreement (on requirements) against certainty (on technology/approach). Close to both agreement and certainty is simple, suggesting predictive delivery; far from both tends toward chaos; the middle ground is complicated or complex, favouring adaptive or hybrid approaches.
sections.tip: Complex and adaptive go together: high uncertainty or low agreement points to iterative, experiment-led delivery.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 205' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><rect x='20' y='16' width='140' height='88' fill='#2F6F5E' fill-opacity='0.13' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><rect x='160' y='16' width='140' height='88' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.13' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><rect x='20' y='104' width='140' height='88' fill='#B5462F' fill-opacity='0.13' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><rect x='160' y='104' width='140' height='88' fill='#C98A1F' fill-opacity='0.13' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><text x='90' y='50' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Complex</text><text x='90' y='66' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>probe-sense-respond</text><text x='230' y='50' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Complicated</text><text x='230' y='66' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>sense-analyse-respond</text><text x='90' y='142' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Chaotic</text><text x='90' y='158' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>act-sense-respond</text><text x='230' y='142' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Clear</text><text x='230' y='158' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>sense-categorise-respond</text><circle cx='160' cy='104' r='23' fill='none' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><text x='160' y='107' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>Disorder</text></svg>
figure.caption: Cynefin: match the response to the kind of problem; Disorder is not yet knowing which domain you are in.

sections.heading: Change models: the people side
sections.body:
These describe how people and organisations move through change, not the technical rollout:
- Lewin: unfreeze (loosen the status quo), change (move to the new way), refreeze (embed it).
- ADKAR: an individual adoption sequence - Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability, Reinforcement. Each person must move through all five.
- Kotter 8-Step: an org-wide sequence from creating urgency, building a coalition and vision, through enabling and short-term wins, to anchoring change in the culture.
- Bridges Transition Model: separates the external change from the internal psychological transition - Ending (letting go), Neutral Zone (the messy in-between), New Beginning. People transition slower than systems change.
- Satir Change Model: late status quo, then resistance, then chaos as performance dips, then integration, then a new status quo. It normalises the temporary dip in performance.
sections.tip: Bridges is about transition (psychology); Lewin and Kotter are about change (the process) - the exam tests that distinction.

sections.heading: Coaching and situational leadership
sections.body:
Situational Leadership II adapts your style to the follower's development level (competence x commitment) on a given task:
- Directing: low competence, high commitment - tell and show.
- Coaching: some competence, low commitment - explain and persuade.
- Supporting: high competence, variable commitment - encourage and share decisions.
- Delegating: high competence, high commitment - hand over responsibility.

GROW is a coaching conversation structure: Goal, Reality (current state), Options, Will (the way forward and commitment).

OSCAR is a related coaching frame: Outcome, Situation, Choices, Actions, Review - useful for longer-term development with built-in follow-up.
sections.tip: There is no single best leadership style - the answer is the one matched to the person and task.

sections.heading: Planning and team-performance models
sections.body:
Boehm's planning sweet spot captures the cost trade-off in planning effort. Too little planning means costly rework and missed risks; too much planning wastes effort and delays delivery, especially under uncertainty. The sweet spot is the level of planning that minimises total cost - and it shifts with how predictable the work is.

Drexler-Sibbet Team Performance Model describes seven stages a team moves through, from orientation and trust building (creating the team) to implementation, high performance and renewal (sustaining the team). It complements Tuckman by adding the resolving questions a team answers at each stage.
sections.tip: On highly uncertain work, plan less up front and re-plan often - the sweet spot moves left.

sections.heading: Where the other models live
sections.body:
To keep this catalogue complete, the models covered in their own guides are:
- Tuckman - team development (forming, storming, norming, performing, adjourning).
- Thomas-Kilmann - conflict modes (competing, collaborating, compromising, avoiding, accommodating).
- Motivation theories - Maslow, Herzberg, McGregor X/Y, McClelland and others.
- Stakeholder salience / Power-Interest - prioritising stakeholders.
- Group Decision-Making - reaching and recording a decision.

Use those guides for depth; use this one to recognise the complexity, change, coaching and planning models at a glance.
sections.tip: In the exam, first name the family (complexity, change, leadership), then pick the specific model.

source: PM Cram study notes — reworded for the current ECO-based PMP exam. Models per PMBOK Guide 7/8.
